samples.label: pricing decision under ownership ambiguity
samples.inputSummary: ownership ambiguity between sales and finance the enterprise pricing change Q3 enterprise renewal revenue Who owns this decision? Unclear — pricing sits between sales and finance What evidence do you have? Churn analysis from March and two enterprise renewal objections What happens if you wait? Competitor undercut continues through Q3
samples.outputText:
The economics of the enterprise pricing change — ownership ambiguity between sales and finance are being contested without a priced hypothesis: value capture, willingness-to-pay, and margin floor are all asserted, none tested, while ownership ambiguity between sales and finance compresses the room to manoeuvre. A secondary ownership_ambiguity signal is present and may become primary if the case evolves.
The tension is margin versus momentum: protecting price risks losing deals now; matching pressure surrenders margin permanently — and the case currently lacks the evidence to price that trade. This reading rests on 6 signals in your answers, not on a generic playbook.
Every month without a priced hypothesis converts willingness-to-pay you could have captured into margin permanently surrendered. In this case the stated exposure is Q3 enterprise renewal revenue.
Write a falsifiable pricing hypothesis — which segment, what value metric, what boundary — and test it on the next ten live deals rather than debating it in the abstract. For the enterprise pricing change — ownership ambiguity between sales and finance, that responsibility sits with Commercial Director as the accountable owner, to be visibly underway within 48 hours.
This judgement classifies the case as pricing_pressure from 6 matched input signals; signals absent from the input could not be weighed. The common false move for this pattern — matching the competitor's price across the board, converting a segmentation problem into a margin problem. — was assumed avoidable; if it is already underway, the sequence above needs re-sequencing.
Escalate when discounting breaches the stated floor twice in one quarter, or when renewal pricing decisions start being made deal-by-deal without a recorded rationale.
Within the next 10 minutes, start the first step: State the pricing hypothesis explicitly: segment, value metric, floor, and walk-away boundary for the enterprise pricing change — ownership ambiguity between sales and finance. Then continue: Apply the hypothesis to the next ten live deals instead of debating it internally; Commercial Director adjudicates exceptions.

samples.label: hiring freeze prioritisation
samples.inputSummary: hiring freeze capacity constraint with no de-scoped workstream engineering delivery plan under the hiring freeze platform launch delivery capacity and customer commitments What is the constraint? Hiring freeze: headcount and capacity cannot grow What evidence do you have? Capacity model shows 140% allocation across five committed workstreams What happens if you wait? The platform launch slips while every workstream degrades together
samples.outputText:
engineering delivery plan under the hiring freeze — hiring freeze capacity constraint with no de-scoped workstream is capacity-bound: the constraint — hiring freeze capacity constraint with no de-scoped workstream — is real, but it is being absorbed as uniform strain across all commitments instead of being converted into an explicit stop-list.
The tension is fairness versus effectiveness: spreading the shortage evenly feels equitable and guarantees everything degrades; concentrating it requires COO to stop work someone champions. This reading rests on 8 signals in your answers, not on a generic playbook.
An unprioritised constraint taxes every initiative at once; the organisation pays full cost for partial delivery everywhere. In this case the stated exposure is platform launch delivery capacity and customer commitments.
Cut scope before cutting corners: rank the active commitments, explicitly stop the bottom of the list, and re-sequence what remains around the scarcest capacity. For engineering delivery plan under the hiring freeze — hiring freeze capacity constraint with no de-scoped workstream, that responsibility sits with COO as the accountable owner, to be visibly underway within 48 hours.
This judgement classifies the case as resource_constraint from 8 matched input signals; signals absent from the input could not be weighed. The common false move for this pattern — spreading the constraint evenly across all work, guaranteeing everything ships late instead of something shipping well. — was assumed avoidable; if it is already underway, the sequence above needs re-sequencing.
Escalate when the constraint forces a choice between commitments made to customers, or when the stop-list requires authority above the current owner.
Within the next 10 minutes, start the first step: Rank every active commitment touched by engineering delivery plan under the hiring freeze — hiring freeze capacity constraint with no de-scoped workstream in a single ordered list — no ties. Then continue: Stop, in writing, the bottom of the list; 'paused' does not count.

samples.label: post-acquisition board disagreement on integration priorities
samples.inputSummary: a mid-market enterprise two quarters into a major acquisition board members disagree on integration pacing — one insists on speed, two push for caution, one abstains acquisition closed; integration planning shows divergent timelines from each board member executive team received conflicting mandates from different board members three integration initiatives are paused awaiting board clarity integration spend is at risk; budget authority hinges on board decision
samples.outputText:
Enterprise governance friction: board members disagree on integration pacing — one insists on speed, two push for caution, one abstains. Pattern classification incomplete from provided evidence (No decision pattern signature reached the minimum evidence threshold in the supplied input.), but the friction itself is clear.
Board disagreement blocks execution, integration timeline slips by quarters, deal value capture erodes, executive team fractures
The CEO (accountable owner) convenes the disagreeing board members or executives for a single decision meeting within 48-hour window. Outcome by end of meeting: write the decision in one sentence. Named owner signs the decision memo. All parties sign commitment to execute by next business day.
Test the hypothesis: "Once we clarify the decision, all parties will act on it consistently." Falsified if: after the decision is written and signed, implementation still diverges or any party refuses to execute.
Escalate to the board or executive team if this friction remains unresolved by integration milestones must restart in 30 days. Define success: "board alignment on integration pacing with written mandate". If not achieved by deadline, governance intervention is required.
Day 1: Executive leadership defines the specific decision in one sentence.
Day 2: Board meets to align on the decision or the decision process.
Day 3: Decision is written and signed by the decision-maker.
Day 4–7: Implementation begins under named accountability.
Day 30: Measure whether implementation is consistent across all parties.
Pattern-specific remediation without full classification.

samples.label: enterprise overstretched: too many initiatives, no clear priorities
samples.inputSummary: a growth-stage enterprise in competitive market with 400+ employees strategic planning approved 14 initiatives this year; no clear win condition per initiative; execution layer is confused about which to prioritize 14 approved strategic initiatives compete for the same engineering resources initiative completion rate is 30% (9 of 30 started last year completed) executive team spends 80% of time re-prioritizing instead of leading execution cross-functional teams report lack of clarity on success metrics per initiative
samples.outputText:
Enterprise governance friction: strategic planning approved 14 initiatives this year; no clear win condition per initiative; execution layer is confused about which to prioritize. Pattern classification incomplete from provided evidence (No decision pattern signature reached the minimum evidence threshold in the supplied input.), but the friction itself is clear.
Initiative overload compounds, execution productivity declines, employee retention at risk, competitive advantage erodes
The CEO (named accountable owner) calls a portfolio reduction meeting tomorrow 10am with the CTO, VP Engineering, and CFO. Outcome by end of day tomorrow: select the top 3 initiatives. By Wednesday end of business: write a decision memo naming one accountable owner per initiative, success metrics, and completion date for each of the top 3. By Thursday end of day: announce the pause decision to all teams and reallocate freed capacity in writing.
Test the hypothesis: "Reducing the initiative portfolio to the top 3 will improve execution velocity and completion rate." Falsified if: after 6 weeks, the completion rate does not improve by at least 15% or team velocity does not increase materially.
Escalate to the board or executive team if this friction remains unresolved by quarterly planning must restart in 45 days. Define success: "ruthlessly prioritized initiative portfolio with clear win conditions". If not achieved by deadline, governance intervention is required.
Week 1: Count active initiatives and measure resource allocation per initiative.
Week 2: Leadership selects the top 3 initiatives with clear win conditions. Pause all others in writing.
Week 3: Communicate pause to teams. Reallocate freed capacity to the top 3.
Week 4–6: Track completion rate and delivery velocity on the top 3.
Week 8: Confirm completion rate improvement (target: 15%+). If <15%, investigate execution blockers.
Pattern-specific remediation without full classification.
